title: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The assumption that a consumer's total utility for a product equals the simple sum of utilities derived from each individual attribute level, with no interaction effects between attributes.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A product characteristic that meaningfully differentiates alternatives in a product category, described in discrete and unambiguous terms.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The omitted reference category for a given attribute in conjoint regression. All other levels' part-worths are measured relative to the baseline, which is assigned a part-worth of zero.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A survey-based quantitative technique that determines how consumers value different product attributes by presenting product profiles and analyzing ratings, rankings, or choices via regression.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A binary (0/1) indicator variable used in regression to represent whether a profile contains a specific attribute level. For an attribute with k levels, k − 1 dummy variables are required.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A conjoint study design in which only a carefully selected subset of all possible attribute level combinations is presented to respondents, used when full factorial designs would create too many profiles.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A conjoint study design in which respondents evaluate every possible combination of attribute levels. Total profiles equals the product of the number of levels across all attributes.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A specific value or category that an attribute can take.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A preference elicitation method in which respondents assign rank numbers to product profiles, producing an ordered list without equal intervals between ranks.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The regression coefficient associated with a specific attribute level, representing that level's contribution to overall product preference relative to the baseline level.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The method by which respondents express product preferences in a conjoint study, including rank-ordering, rating scales, choice between pairs, and hybrid adaptive methods.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A specific product concept defined by one level for each attribute. In a full factorial design, the number of profiles equals the product of all attribute level counts.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: A preference elicitation method in which respondents score each product profile on a numerical scale, allowing cardinal comparison of preferences.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The statistical technique used to estimate part-worth utilities in conjoint analysis. The dependent variable is the preference score or rank; independent variables are dummy-coded attribute levels.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The percentage of total utility variation attributable to each attribute, calculated as an attribute's part-worth range divided by the sum of all attributes' ranges.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: An alternative name for conjoint analysis, emphasizing that respondents must implicitly trade off desirable attribute levels against undesirable ones when expressing preferences for product profiles.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems

cards.definition: The maximum additional price a consumer would pay to obtain an attribute improvement, estimated by dividing the attribute's part-worth by the price coefficient per dollar in the conjoint regression.
cards.sourceLecture: Practice Problems: Conjoint Analysis Practice Problems
